title: Consistent hashing and virtual nodes
markdown:
## Why this matters

Any system that spreads keys across N nodes — caches, distributed databases, message partitions — must answer: what happens when N changes? Naive `hash(key) mod N` remaps nearly every key when a node joins or leaves, which at scale means a self-inflicted cache wipe or a rebalancing storm. Consistent hashing is the standard fix and a building block you will reuse in countless designs.

## Core mechanics

### The ring
Map the hash space (say 0 to 2^64-1) onto a conceptual ring. Hash each node onto the ring; hash each key onto the ring; a key belongs to the first node clockwise from it. When a node joins, it takes keys only from its clockwise successor; when it leaves, its keys go only to its successor. Expected fraction of keys that move: K/N — the theoretical minimum.

### The variance problem
With one point per node, the gaps between nodes are wildly uneven — some nodes own several times their fair share. Worse, a departing node dumps its entire range onto one successor.

### Virtual nodes
Hash each physical node onto the ring at many points (typically 100-1000 "vnodes"). Benefits: (1) load variance shrinks roughly with the square root of the vnode count; (2) a leaving node's ranges scatter across many successors, spreading the rebalancing load; (3) heterogeneous hardware gets proportional vnode counts (a 2x machine gets 2x vnodes). Cost: the ring's routing table grows (N × vnodes entries), and range scans across owners get more fragmented.

### Implementation sketch
A sorted array or tree of (hash, node) pairs; lookup = binary search for the first entry ≥ hash(key), wrapping at the end. O(log(N·V)) per lookup, trivially cacheable.

### Alternatives worth knowing
- **Rendezvous (HRW) hashing**: for each key, score every node with hash(key, node) and pick the max. No ring, perfectly uniform, minimal movement — but O(N) per lookup unless layered.
- **Jump consistent hash**: O(1), zero memory, beautifully uniform — but only supports numbered buckets added/removed at the end, so it fits storage shard counts, not arbitrary node churn.
- **Maglev hashing**: Google's lookup-table variant optimized for load balancers — O(1) lookups with minimal disruption on backend changes.

## Trade-offs and failure modes

- Vnode count is a dial: more vnodes = smoother distribution but bigger routing tables and more fragmented ranges. Cassandra's default moved from 256 to 16 as clusters grew — high vnode counts hurt repair and streaming.
- Consistent hashing balances *key counts*, not *load*: one celebrity key still overwhelms its owner (hot partition). Fixes live above the ring: key salting, request-level caching, or splitting hot keys.
- Ring membership must itself be consistent: if two routers disagree about membership (gossip lag), the same key routes to different nodes — design for brief divergence (read repair, hinted handoff).

## Real-world example

Amazon's Dynamo paper popularized the ring with vnodes; Cassandra and Riak inherited it. DynamoDB-style systems pair the ring with replication: a key is stored on its owner plus the next R-1 distinct physical nodes clockwise (skipping vnodes of the same machine — an easy bug to introduce).

## Interview framing

Lead with the mod-N failure ("adding one node remaps (N-1)/N of all keys"), then the ring, then immediately volunteer vnodes — interviewers wait to see if you know single-point rings are unbalanced. Close with the hot-key caveat: consistent hashing solves *placement*, not *load skew*.

## Key takeaways

- mod-N remaps almost everything on membership change; the ring remaps only K/N keys.
- Virtual nodes fix load variance and scatter rebalancing across the cluster.
- Replication must skip vnodes of the same physical machine.
- Consistent hashing balances placement, not load — hot keys need separate treatment.
key_takeaways: hash(key) mod N remaps nearly all keys when N changes; a hash ring remaps only K/N.
Virtual nodes shrink load variance and spread a departed node's range across many successors.
Vnode count trades distribution smoothness against routing-table size and range fragmentation.
Replica placement must skip vnodes belonging to the same physical node.
Consistent hashing solves placement, not load skew — celebrity keys still create hot partitions.

questions.prompt: A node leaves a consistent-hash ring that has NO virtual nodes. What happens to its keys?
choices: They are redistributed evenly across all remaining nodes
They all move to the single clockwise successor, doubling its load
They are lost until the node returns
Half go to the predecessor and half to the successor
questions.correct_answer: They all move to the single clockwise successor, doubling its load
questions.explanation: Without vnodes, one node owns one contiguous arc, and the whole arc transfers to its successor — the rebalancing hotspot that virtual nodes exist to fix.

questions.prompt: Why does consistent hashing NOT solve the hot partition (celebrity key) problem?
choices: Because it balances where keys live, not how much traffic each key receives
Because hot keys hash to multiple nodes simultaneously
Because virtual nodes concentrate hot keys
It does solve it, by spreading requests across replicas
questions.correct_answer: Because it balances where keys live, not how much traffic each key receives
questions.explanation: Placement uniformity and load uniformity are different problems: one key with 100k req/s still lands on one owner. Salting, caching, or key splitting address load skew.

questions.prompt: Your cluster replicates each key to 3 nodes by walking clockwise from the key's position and taking the next 3 vnodes. What bug does this have and how do you fix it?
questions.correct_answer: Consecutive vnodes can belong to the same physical machine, so the three 'replicas' may land on fewer than three physical nodes, silently reducing fault tolerance. Fix: walk clockwise taking vnodes but skip any whose physical node (or rack/zone for stronger placement) is already in the replica set, until you have 3 distinct machines.
questions.explanation: The distinct-physical-node (and ideally distinct-rack) constraint is the classic vnode replication gotcha from the Dynamo lineage.
